name: Chloe Wright

participation_history.notes: Great session. Chloe walked us through her full checkout experience with real examples.

notes: She/her. Shops online 3-4x per week, mostly on mobile. Highly opinionated about checkout UX — has abandoned many carts. Very articulate participant.

session_insights.raw_notes: Chloe shared that she almost always abandons checkout if she has to create an account. Said she has 'account fatigue' from too many sign-ups. The guest checkout option on our app is hidden below the login form which she never noticed - she thought it wasn't possible. Big moment: when asked to complete a test purchase she spent 40 seconds looking for an apple pay or google pay button - said 'I never type my card details on my phone, it's too fiddly and I don't trust it'. Also flagged that the order summary disappears when you scroll down on the payment screen - she lost track of what she was buying. Quote: 'If I have to type my card number on a tiny keyboard I'm already annoyed before I've bought anything'. Said she'd recommend the app to friends if checkout was faster.
key_insights: Guest checkout option is invisible — placed below the login form, users assume it doesn't exist
Strong preference for one-tap payment (Apple Pay / Google Pay) on mobile — typing card details feels fiddly and untrustworthy
Order summary should remain visible (sticky) during the payment step — users lose context when it disappears on scroll
Account creation fatigue is real — forced sign-up is the primary abandonment trigger for frequent online shoppers
follow_up_items: Redesign guest checkout CTA — make it equal prominence to the login option
Prioritise Apple Pay / Google Pay integration in the next sprint
Make order summary sticky on the payment screen
Chloe is happy to review a redesigned prototype — flag for concept testing round
quotes: If I have to type my card number on a tiny keyboard I'm already annoyed before I've bought anything
I didn't even know guest checkout was an option — I thought you had to sign up

name: Marcus Thompson
notes: He/him. Shops online once or twice a month, mostly for considered purchases. Reads reviews carefully before buying. Good contrast to power-user participants.

name: Anika Patel
notes: She/her. 22 years old. Shops almost exclusively on mobile. Price-compares across apps before buying. Heavy social commerce user (TikTok Shop, Instagram).

name: David Morrison
notes: He/him. 58 years old. Uses a tablet, not a smartphone, for most online shopping. Values clear trust signals (padlocks, brand names). Represents an accessibility and digital confidence angle.

name: Jasmine Lee
persona: Power user — shops across many categories
notes: She/her. Shops online 5+ times per week across fashion, tech, and home. Has strong opinions about returns and refund processes. Good for post-purchase experience research.

name: Noah Fitzgerald
notes: He/him. Tries new apps early. Notices technical details others miss — broken animations, slow load states, inconsistent UI. Useful for finding edge cases.

name: Priya Sharma
notes: She/her. Parent of 3. Shops in short bursts between tasks — speed and saved preferences matter a lot. Frequently uses wish lists and saved carts.

name: Tom Adeyemi
notes: Withdrew consent after the February survey and asked to be removed from the panel.

name: Emma Larsson
persona: Thoughtful shopper, values trust and simplicity
notes: She/her. Shops deliberately — researches before buying. Cares about data privacy and clear returns policies. Good for testing information hierarchy and trust-building content.

name: Carlos Mendez
notes: He/him. Buys on impulse from social media ads. Gets frustrated when the path from ad to checkout is too long. Good for testing social commerce and quick-buy flows.

name: Lily Park
notes: She/her. Has a strong eye for visual design — notices inconsistencies in UI instantly. Shops heavily in fashion and home categories. Valuable for concept testing and visual design feedback.

name: Robert Okafor
notes: He/him. Shops online for practical household and work items. Cares about delivery speed and simple reordering. Represents a no-frills, task-oriented user type.
